A low pressure system that moved through the Delmarva Peninsula coastal waters brought heavy snow to Sussex County and a wintry mix of precipitation across the rest of Northwest New Jersey.  Snow began across the region during the morning on the 10th.  Enough warm air at higher levels of the atmosphere moved into the area for precipitation to change over to a rain and freezing rain mixture by sunset on the 10th, except for Sussex County where precipitation remained mainly snow.  The heaviest precipitation was over by midnight on the 10th, but lighter snow and/or flurries lingered until around sunrise on the 11th.   Snowfall accumulations averaged around 6 inches in Sussex County and 1 to 3 inches elsewhere.  The wintry precipitation caused accidents throughout the area, although no serious ones were reported. Specific accumulations included 6 inches in Wantage and Sussex (Sussex County), 5.5 inches in Montague (Sussex County), 5 inches in Newton (Sussex County),  3 inches in Long Valley (Morris County), 2.4 inches in Chatham (Morris County), 2 inches in Boonton (Morris County) and Pottersville (Somerset County) and 0.8 inches in Flemington (Hunterdon County) and Stewartsville (Warren County).

A low pressure system developed over southern Texas the morning of the 23rd and moved northeast and reached southern Missouri the morning of the 24th and extreme southwest Ontario the morning of the 25th.  A second low pressure system did form along the Delmarva Peninsula and was near the New Jersey Coast on the morning of the 25th.  This second low stalled the southerly flow of warm air enough to have precipitation start briefly as freezing rain in a few of the deeper valleys in northwestern Morris County and Sussex County.

Part of a 90-foot-tall tree fell on top of a unit of the Bayview Condominium in Highlands (Monmouth County).  Numerous power lines were reported down in Long Branch (Monmouth County).  GPU Energy reported about 6,300 homes and businesses lost power from Middletown Township in Monmouth County south through Toms River in Ocean County.  Peak wind gusts included 61 mph in Brighton Beach and 57 mph in Harvey Cedars (both in Ocean County).  Farther south along the shore, the rain was not as heavy (especially in Cape May County) and did not coincide as well with the high tide.  The highest tides that evening included 6.9 feet above mean low water in Cape May, 6.4 feet above mean low water in Atlantic City, 5.2 feet above mean low water in Sea Bright and 3.1 feet above mean low water in Ship Bottom on Manahawkin Bay.  Tidal departures were between 2.1 and 2.8 feet above normal at the times of high tide.  Heavy rain also fell throughout a large part of the state, particularly during the evening of the 29th.  Storm totals averaged around 2.00 inches and caused the typical urban and poor drainage flooding.  Hardest hit were the Raritan Basin counties of Hunterdon, Middlesex and Somerset.  Several streets "notorious" for flooding in those counties were closed, but the main stem rivers stayed within their banks.  Storm totals included 2.8 inches in Lakehurst (Ocean County), 2.73 inches in Margate (Atlantic County), 2.1 inches in New Lisbon (Burlington County), 2.08 inches in Iselin (Middlesex County), 1.96 inches in Freehold (Monmouth County), 1.77 inches in Boonton (Morris County), 1.69 inches in Hightstown (Mercer County), 1.6 inches in Belle Mead (Somerset County) and Wertsville (Hunterdon County).  Storm totals from Camden County south were generally under an inch.

A low pressure system formed to the lee of the Southern Rockies the morning of December 28th and moved east into the Tennessee Valley the morning of the 29th.  A  second low pressure system formed along the South Carolina Coast the morning of the 29th and intensified rapidly as it moved northward and became the main low pressure system.  The low moved inland along the New Jersey shore the night of the 29th and entered the state close to the Little Egg Harbor Inlet and proceeded north along the coastal counties.  By 7 a.m. EST on the 30th it was located in southern Vermont.This low pressure system brought heavy rain to a large part of the state, high winds to Monmouth and Ocean Counties as it was intensifying and some minor tidal flooding at the times of the evening high tide on the 29th.  The heavy rain in most places coincided with the incoming tide and this exacerbated the poor drainage flooding along the coastal communities.  In the New Jersey coastal counties, in Monmouth County,  the heavy rain caused the collapse of a home in the Stonehurst Development in Freehold Township.  The basement wall gave in and the home was deemed uninhabitable.  The combination of the heavy rain and tides caused flooding and road closures in Highlands, Keyport, Monmouth Beach,  Port Monmouth, Sea Bright and Union Beach.  Union Beach was one of the harder hit communities with barricades all over town.  In Hazlet, the heavy rain forced the closure of sections of New Jersey State Route 36.  In Ocean County, the tides and heavy rain forced the closure of several main roadways and one traffic circle in Long Beach Township, Ship Bottom and Surf City.  In addition to the heavy rain and tides, wind gusts reached around 60 mph and knocked down trees and power lines.  Part of a 90-foot-tall tree fell on top of a unit of the Bayview Condominium in Highlands (Monmouth County).  Numerous power lines were reported down in Long Branch (Monmouth County).  GPU Energy reported about 6,300 homes and businesses lost power from Middletown Township in Monmouth County south through Toms River in Ocean County.  Peak wind gusts included 61 mph in Brighton Beach and 57 mph in Harvey Cedars (both in Ocean County).  Farther south along the shore, the rain was not as heavy (especially in Cape May County) and did not coincide as well with the high tide.  The highest tides that evening included 6.9 feet above mean low water in Cape May, 6.4 feet above mean low water in Atlantic City, 5.2 feet above mean low water in Sea Bright and 3.1 feet above mean low water in Ship Bottom on Manahawkin Bay.  Tidal departures were between 2.1 and 2.8 feet above normal at the times of high tide.  Heavy rain also fell throughout a large part of the state, particularly during the evening of the 29th.  Storm totals averaged around 2.00 inches and caused the typical urban and poor drainage flooding.  Hardest hit were the Raritan Basin counties of Hunterdon, Middlesex and Somerset.  Several streets "notorious" for flooding in those counties were closed, but the main stem rivers stayed within their banks.  Storm totals included 2.8 inches in Lakehurst (Ocean County), 2.73 inches in Margate (Atlantic County), 2.1 inches in New Lisbon (Burlington County), 2.08 inches in Iselin (Middlesex County), 1.96 inches in Freehold (Monmouth County), 1.77 inches in Boonton (Morris County), 1.69 inches in Hightstown (Mercer County), 1.6 inches in Belle Mead (Somerset County) and Wertsville (Hunterdon County).  Storm totals from Camden County south were generally under an inch.

A low pressure system that moved through the Delmarva Peninsula coastal waters brought heavy snow to Sussex County and a wintry mix of precipitation across the rest of Northwest New Jersey.  Snow began across the region during the morning on the 10th.  Enough warm air at higher levels of the atmosphere moved into the area for precipitation to change over to a rain and freezing rain mixture by sunset on the 10th, except for Sussex County where precipitation remained mainly snow.  The heaviest precipitation was over by midnight on the 10th, but lighter snow and/or flurries lingered until around sunrise on the 11th.   Snowfall accumulations averaged around 6 inches in Sussex County and 1 to 3 inches elsewhere.  The wintry precipitation caused accidents throughout the area, although no serious ones were reported. Specific accumulations included 6 inches in Wantage and Sussex (Sussex County), 5.5 inches in Montague (Sussex County), 5 inches in Newton (Sussex County),  3 inches in Long Valley (Morris County), 2.4 inches in Chatham (Morris County), 2 inches in Boonton (Morris County) and Pottersville (Somerset County) and 0.8 inches in Flemington (Hunterdon County) and Stewartsville (Warren County).

A high pressure system moved across the Middle Atlantic and New England States on the 21st and 22nd.  It brought dry and cold air into the region.  Early Monday morning (the 22nd) a secondary low pressure system was developing off the South Carolina Coast as its primary system was weakening in the Tennessee Valley.  The South Carolina low intensified and moved quickly up the Eastern Seaboard and was just south of Long Island at 7 a.m. EST on the 23rd.  While warmer air arrived across New Jersey above the ground, the low moved too quickly (coupled with a land-based northeast flow trajectory) to permit surface temperatures to get above freezing,  This was especially true in Warren and Sussex Counties.  Precipitation started briefly as light snow and sleet the afternoon of the 22nd, but once it intensified during the night time hours of the 22nd and 23rd, it fell mainly as freezing rain.  Temperatures did slowly rise overnight and most locations in Hunterdon, Mercer, Morris and Somerset Counties were above the freezing mark by the morning of the 23rd.  An inch or less of snow fell across the area and between a quarter to a half an inch of ice accrued on exposed surfaces.  Driving was hazardous on untreated roadways and the freezing rain contributed to numerous accidents, although no very serious ones were reported.

Lightning helped ignite a fire in the second story of a two story building in  Asbury Park.  The lightning apparently struck the roof and the fire smouldered unnoticed for about an hour.  The fire did cause moderate damage to the upstairs apartment as well as water damage throughout the building.  No one was injured.

A line of heavy showers with thunderstorms produced from 1 to 2 inches of rain in 1 hour. This rainfall was preceded by a 1 to 2 inch rainfall during the past 24 hours.Water rapidly accumulated to a depth up to 4 feet in low-lying and poor drainage areas, stranding many motorists, flooding basements, and blowing open manhole covers.  Jersey City and Secaucus where particularly hard hit.

The weekend of August 16th and 17th brought some of the hottest and most humid air of the summer into Central and Southern New Jersey.   High temperatures in most places reached well into the 90s, while dew point temperatures (an indication of how humid the air mass is) were as high as the mid 70s.  The apparent temperature or heat index on the 16th reached around 110 degrees.   The highest temperatures this weekend (on Saturday the 16th for most locations) included 100 degrees in Brick (Ocean County), 99 degrees at the McGuire AFB (Burlington County),  97 degrees in Somerdale (Camden County) and also at the Marina in Atlantic City on the 17th which was a new record,  96 degrees at the Atlantic City International Airport (on the 16th which tied a record) and 95 degrees in New Brunswick (Middlesex County), Mount Holly (Burlington County) and Wertsville (Hunterdon County).   The hot spell ended abruptly as a strong cold front moved through the region late on the 17th.  No serious heat related illnesses were reported.

Showers and thunderstorms with heavy rain caused some urban and poor drainage flooding in Northwest New Jersey.  The heaviest rain fell in two bursts: during the morning commute hours and again during the mid afternoon.  Storm totals averaged between 2.0 and 3.5 inches.  Two children were injured in Byram Township when a rotten tree limb from a dead tree fell onto them while they were waiting for a school bus.  Storm totals included 4.33 inches in Montague (Sussex County),  3.53 inches in Morristown (Morris County), 3.28 inches in both Rockaway (Morris County)  and White House Station (Hunterdon County),  2.91 inches in Oak Ridge (Morris County),  2.81 inches in Wantage (Sussex County),  2.53 inches in Long Valley (Morris County),  2.35 inches in Chatham (Morris County), 2.28 inches in Belvidere (Warren County) and 2.07 inches in Sussex (Sussex County).

A vigorous cold front moved through New Jersey during the early morning on the 27th and was accompanied by strong gusty winds.  The strongest gusts occurred during two surges,  one within a couple of hours of the cold frontal passage.  The second (and in most areas just as strong) surge occurred during the late morning on the 27th.  At about 1120 a.m. EST, a 31-year-old woman was killed when she was struck in the head by a falling tree limb and knocked off her horse during a fox hunt on the Ellisdale Road property of Princeton Nurseries in Upper Freehold Township in Monmouth County.  The strong winds pulled down tree limbs and this resulted in power outages to about 20,000 homes throughout the northern half of New Jersey.  Some of the hardest hit areas included Newton (Sussex County) and Flemington (Hunterdon County).  Peak wind gusts included 55 mph in Harvey Cedars (Ocean County)  and at the McGuire AFB (Burlington County) and 53 mph in Belmar (Monmouth County) and Chatham (Morris County).  While it was also windy in the southern third of New Jersey, the highest wind gusts were a bit lower, generally between 35 mph and 45 mph.

With a strong high pressure system extending from north of New England southwest across the Great Lakes, a low pressure system formed off the Southeast Coast during Thursday, November 6th.  The low slowly intensified as it moved very slowly north off the Mid-Atlantic Coast Friday and Saturday.  It did not pass north of the area until Sunday, November 9th.The heaviest rain fell during Saturday morning and afternoon on November 8th.  Generally 1 1/2 to 2 1/2 inches of rain fell across the area.  However, specific amounts ranged from 1.00 inches at Newark Airport (Essex County) to 2.74 inches at Woodcliff Lake  (Bergen County). Minor flooding of low-lying poor drainage areas occurred where leafs clogged drains.  In addition, northeast winds gusted from 25 to 35 mph.

Frequent lightning caused numerous power outages in Northern Ocean County.  The Toms River area of Dover Township was hardest hit.  Outages were also reported in Berkeley and Manchester Townships and also in Seaside Heights.  During the height of the storm about 10,500 GPU customers lost power.  The storm caused a power outage at the Community Medical Center on New Jersey State Route 37 and forced the hospital to run on its backup generators for about two hours.

A coastal storm developed off the Carolina Coast during Saturday morning, November 1st.  It moved north and passed over the Delaware Coast around 3 pm and over the local area during the evening.The storm brought strong gusty winds and rain to the area.East winds increased to 20 to 30 mph with gusts from 40 to 45 mph during the afternoon.  The highest wind gust was 44 mph at West Orange (Essex County).  Strong gusty winds combined with rain and downed scattered tree limbs, trees, and power lines.  An 85-foot oak tree fell and damaged 3 fences and a house deck on Franklin Road in Englewood (Bergen County).Rainfall amounts ranged from 0.82 inches at Newark Airport  to 1.83 inches at West Orange (Essex  County).

The same thunderstorm that produced the frequent lightning was also accompanied by torrential downpours.  National Weather Service Doppler Radar estimated  around 3 inches of rain fell within one hour over parts of Northern Ocean County.  This caused urban and small stream flooding, mainly within Dover Township.

A gust front from a squall line of severe thunderstorms knocked down trees in Edison.  As the thunderstorms themselves moved across central Middlesex County, they reintensified and proceeded to cause scattered power outages mainly in the eastern half of the county as they downed more trees and wires. About 35,000 homes and businesses in the Public Service Electric and Gas service area in central New Jersey lost power on the 16th; 4,000 still did not have power restored by the evening of the 17th.

A squall line of severe thunderstorms caused widespread tree damage, mainly in the northeast half,  and killed one person in Morris County.  A 53 year-old-male from Denville died of a heart attack after being hospitalized for injuries sustained from being hit by a four foot wide tree limb while retrieving the mail.  Morris County was the second hardest hit county in northwest New Jersey this afternoon.  There were so many downed wires along New Jersey State Route 10  that it looked like spaghetti to observers.  Trees fell across and blocked the southbound lanes of Interstate 287.  Mountain Lakes Borough had 36 reports of downed trees.  In Montville Township, a 100-year-old tree punched a hole in the roof of one home.  In Boonton Township one house was so badly damaged by fallen trees that authorities feared it might have to be condemned.  Numerous downed trees and wires were also reported in Chatham, Denville, Montville and Rockaway.  This squall line caused numerous power outages throughout the northern half of New Jersey as falling trees took down electrical wires.  About 97,000 GPU customers lost power that day and 16,000 customers in Sussex and Morris Counties still did not have power restored on the morning of the 18th.  Three thousand of these customers were in Montville Township.

A squall line of severe thunderstorms knocked down trees and power lines throughout the county.  But the majority of the wind damage occurred near and along the Delaware River, especially within the Delaware Water Gap National Recreational Area.  A measured wind gust of 60 mph along with dime size hail was reported from Washington Borough.  Marble size hail was reported in Blairstown and downed wires closed sections of New Jersey State Route 94 within the township.   This squall line caused numerous power outages throughout the northern half of New Jersey as falling trees took down electrical wires.  About 97,000 GPU customers lost power that day.

An intense low pressure system that moved east of Cape Hatteras in combination with relatively high astronomical tides coming off the full moon on Wednesday the 15th produced minor tidal flooding around the time of the midday high tide on the 19th.  Two low pressure systems formed off the South Atlantic Coast on the 18th: one near Cape Hatteras, the other just southeast of the South Carolina Coast.  They merged into one during the morning of the 19th east of Cape Hatteras.  During the next 24 hours, this low deepened rapidly (or bombed) from 1000 mb the morning of the 19th to 976 mb the morning of the 20th.  Fortunately for the New Jersey coast, this low moved eastnortheast and not northeast.  By the morning of the 20th, it was already 780 miles east of the New Jersey coastline.     The highest tides associated with this low occurred during the midday high tide on Sunday the 19th. This caused some minor tidal flooding.  The high tide in Atlantic City was 7.0 feet above mean low water and in Wildwood 7.5 feet above mean low water.  Several streets in North Wildwood were closed because of the tidal flooding.  The persistent northeast flow for a couple of days also caused rough surf and some beach erosion.  The United States Coast Guard rescued a single male about 3/4 of a mile off the Ocean City coast.

Strong east winds preceding and accompanying a low pressure system that moved north from Cape Hatteras North Carolina through New Jersey during the day on the 1st knocked over some weak trees in Middlesex County and pulled down some wires in Burlington County.  In Middlesex County, in East Brunswick, an uprooted tree took down wires on Ainsworth Avenue and subsequently caused a power outage to about 200 homes.  New Jersey State Route 27 was blocked by fallen trees in northern parts of Middlesex County.  Peak wind gusts during the day were close to 40 mph at both the Atlantic City and Newark International Airports.

New Jersey State Route 27 was blocked by fallen trees in northern parts of Middlesex County.  Peak wind gusts during the day were close to 40 mph at both the Atlantic City and Newark International Airports.

A squall line of severe thunderstorms knocked down scores of trees and wires throughout Hunterdon County.  One tree fell on and seriously injured a person.  A SKYWARN Spotter reported a measured wind gust of 60 mph in Flemington.  The hardest hit locations included Clinton, Flemington, Glen Gardner, Quakertown, Raritan and Ringoes.  This squall line caused numerous power outages throughout the northern half of New Jersey as falling trees took down electrical wires.  About 97,000 GPU customers lost power that day.

A measured wind gust of 60 mph along with dime size hail was reported from Washington Borough.  Marble size hail was reported in Blairstown and downed wires closed sections of New Jersey State Route 94 within the township.   This squall line caused numerous power outages throughout the northern half of New Jersey as falling trees took down electrical wires.  About 97,000 GPU customers lost power that day.

Torrential rain fell across southeast New Jersey as a low pressure system developed over the Delmarva Peninsula and slowly moved northeast across southern New Jersey.  A series of thunderstorms developed along this low pressure system's frontal boundaries and trained or moved over the same areas.  Cape May County escaped the brunt of the torrential downpours,  but Doppler Radar storm total estimates (which worked pretty well with this event)  were between 8 to 10 inches across the northwest part of the county. Measured storm totals included 9.16 inches in Belleplain and 6.82 inches in Dennis Township.   Rainfall amounts diminished drastically toward the southern end of the county.  The storm total estimates were only between 1.0 and 1.5 inches in Cape May City.  The torrential downpours caused back pressure for the streams emptying into the Tuckahoe River and subsequently caused 65 feet of Weatherbee Road (or County Road 548) to wash out.  Part of County Route 550 in Dennis Township was also closed due to flooding.  Repairs to Weatherbee Road cost $485,000.

Torrential rain fell across southeast New Jersey as a low pressure system developed over the Delmarva Peninsula and slowly moved northeast across southern New Jersey.  A series of thunderstorms developed along this low pressure system's frontal boundaries and trained or moved over the same areas. This caused  torrential downpours to fall for several hours across southern and eastern parts of Cumberland County.  Storm totals included 10.12 inches in Millville, 8 inches in Cedarville and 7.2 inches in Vineland.  Doppler Radar storm total estimates showed 8 to 10 inches prevailed across much of Maurice River Township.  The torrential rain caused flash flooding of many rivers and streams in spite of the sandy soil.  In Vineland, the heavy rain and flooding along the Menantico Creek washed out two bridges: on Hance Bridge Road (County Route 673) and on Mays Landing Road (County Route 522).  The bridges were repaired and reopened about one month later.  Large sections of New Jersey State Routes 49 and 55 were closed.   In Millville, the Prudential Building had 5 to 6 feet of water in its basement.  Five major roads within the city were closed.  Sharp Street suffered major erosion.  Hankins Pond overflowed and flooded the parking lot of the Millville Senior School.  The gage house along the Menantico Creek collapsed.   In Maurice River Township, sections of New Jersey State Route 49 were also closed. Total damage was estimated at about 2.1 million dollars.  Miraculously, no serious injuries were reported.

With a high pressure system extending from northwest of the Great Lakes east-southeast across Northern New England, a low pressure system developed off the Carolina Coast during Thursday evening on November 13th.  It slowly intensified as it moved northeast, passing south of Long Island during Friday afternoon before moving offshore during Friday evening.A mixture of snow, sleet, and freezing rain created hazardous weather conditions across the area.  Precipitation accumulated on roads, making both driving and walking hazardous.  Several, mostly minor, traffic accidents occurred.  Freezing rain also accumulated on trees, causing some tree limbs to fall on power lines that resulted in scattered power outages.

An intense low pressure system that moved east of Cape Hatteras in combination with relatively high astronomical tides coming off the full moon on Wednesday the 15th produced minor tidal flooding around the time of the midday high tide on the 19th.  Two low pressure systems formed off the South Atlantic Coast on the 18th: one near Cape Hatteras, the other just southeast of the South Carolina Coast.  They merged into one during the morning of the 19th east of Cape Hatteras.  During the next 24 hours, this low deepened rapidly (or bombed) from 1000 mb the morning of the 19th to 976 mb the morning of the 20th.  Fortunately for the New Jersey coast, this low moved eastnortheast and not northeast.  By the morning of the 20th, it was already 780 miles east of the New Jersey coastline.     The highest tides associated with this low occurred during the midday high tide on Sunday the 19th. This caused some minor tidal flooding.  The high tide in Atlantic City was 7.0 feet above mean low water and in Wildwood 7.5 feet above mean low water.  Several streets in North Wildwood were closed because of the tidal flooding.  The persistent northeast flow for a couple of days also caused rough surf and some beach erosion.  The United States Coast Guard rescued a single male about 3/4 of a mile off the Ocean City coast.

A squall line of severe thunderstorms caused widespread tree damage and killed one person in sussex County.  Most of the wind damage occurred in the northeast half of the county.   Other reports of downed trees in the rest of the county came mainly from Greendell and Stillwater Township. A 64-year-old man was killed by a falling tree outside of his home in Green Township.  Franklin Borough was one of the hardest hit locations within the county.  A limited state of emergency was declared.  New Jersey State Route 23 was closed as were five other major roadways within the borough.  Several trees fell on and damaged homes.  Siding was ripped from a few homes and a couple of decks and railings were ripped away from other homes.  Part of Hardin Lumber's roof was blown away.  In addition to the wind damage, the frequent lightning ignited two houses on fire.  Sussex Borough was also hard hit and also declared a state of emergency.  Six major roads were closed because of fallen trees, some through the 17th.  A couple of planes at Sussex County Airport were flipped over.  Pieces of these planes were found in the woods.  Trees as high as 150 feet were uprooted.  In Sparta Township, two major roads were also closed (including County Road 517).  Other locations that reported wind damage in the county included Fredon Township,  Hopatcong,  Lafayette Township, Vernon and Wantage.   In Vernon, the damaging winds wreaked havoc at the Vernon Day celebration and scattered debris all over Vernon Crossings Fields.  This squall line caused numerous power outages throughout the northern half of New Jersey as falling trees took down electrical wires.  About 97,000 GPU customers lost power that day in the northern half of the state.  About 16,000 customers in Sussex and Morris Counties still did not have power restored to them on the morning of the 18th.

A squall line of severe thunderstorms knocked down scores of trees and wires throughout Hunterdon County.  One tree fell on and seriously injured a person.  A SKYWARN Spotter reported a measured wind gust of 60 mph in Flemington.  The hardest hit locations included Clinton, Flemington, Glen Gardner, Quakertown, Raritan and Ringoes.  This squall line caused numerous power outages throughout the northern half of New Jersey as falling trees took down electrical wires.  About 97,000 GPU customers lost power that day.

Lines of heavy showers and thunderstorms developed in a moist unstable airmass ahead of a slow moving cold front.  As the lines moved slowly north, a thunderstorm produced a weak (F1)  short-lived (about 30 seconds)  tornado in West Milford township.The tornado formed in the Wonder Lake area just east of Germantown Road (off Route 23 North) and moved north.  It  was confirmed by National Weather Service Meteorologists.High winds downed about 150 trees, two of which fell through a house and the Wonder Lake Clubhouse.  At least 4 trees fell on and damaged cars.  High winds also downed numerous power lines.Heavy rain also caused flooding of streets and poor drainage areas.  Here are selected rainfall amounts for Western Passaic County:Charlotteburg      - 4.83 inchesGreenwood Lake - 4.34 inchesWest Milford       - 4.18 inches.

With a strong high pressure system extending from north of New England southwest across the Great Lakes, a low pressure system formed off the Southeast Coast during Thursday, November 6th.  The low slowly intensified as it moved very slowly north off the Mid-Atlantic Coast Friday and Saturday.  It did not pass north of the area until Sunday, November 9th.The heaviest rain fell during Saturday morning and afternoon on November 8th.  Generally 1 1/2 to 2 1/2 inches of rain fell across the area.  However, specific amounts ranged from 1.00 inches at Newark Airport (Essex County) to 2.74 inches at Woodcliff Lake  (Bergen County). Minor flooding of low-lying poor drainage areas occurred where leafs clogged drains.  In addition, northeast winds gusted from 25 to 35 mph.

The same severe thunderstorm that caused wind damage in Delaware County and Philadelphia also produced some wind damage in Woodbury.  The worst damage occurred on North Evergreen Street as six large trees were either snapped or uprooted.  An apartment building on the street was struck by two trees.  A recently bought house sustained moderate damage as a 50 foot tall twin trunk tree became embedded in its dormer window.  Damage to the home was estimated at $50,000.  Trees and wires were also reported down on five other blocks in Woodbury, but the damage was not as severe.  No injuries were reported.
A severe thunderstorm pulled down wires in Brick Township.

Unseasonably dry weather with below normal rainfall, which became worse during the summer months, forced the Delaware River Basin Commission to declare a drought warning on October 27th.  The commission urged the 7 million residents within the basin's 13,539 square mile area to voluntarily conserve water.  Water levels in the New York City Reservoirs, which are in the headwaters of the Delaware River, fell below 40 percent of capacity in late October.  Also, the salt line in the Delaware River, was located near Bridgeport in Gloucester County, approximately four miles farther north than it should have been for late October.  The yearly precipitation totals on a county weighted average were below normal in all of New Jersey except for Atlantic, Cape May and Cumberland Counties.  Precipitation deficits through October 31st averaged around 5 inches in the other counties.  The greatest deficits were in Salem (-7.2 inches), Morris (-7.1 inches) and Somerset (-6.0 inches) Counties.  In the latter three counties, this was about 80 percent of the normal precipitation.  Because a large number of New Jersey farms have irrigation capabilities, agricultural losses from the drought were not as severe as they were in Maryland and Pennsylvania.

A vigorous cold front moved through New Jersey during the early morning on the 27th and was accompanied by strong gusty winds.  The strongest gusts occurred during two surges,  one within a couple of hours of the cold frontal passage.  The second (and in most areas just as strong) surge occurred during the late morning on the 27th.  At about 1120 a.m. EST, a 31-year-old woman was killed when she was struck in the head by a falling tree limb and knocked off her horse during a fox hunt on the Ellisdale Road property of Princeton Nurseries in Upper Freehold Township in Monmouth County.  The strong winds pulled down tree limbs and this resulted in power outages to about 20,000 homes throughout the northern half of New Jersey.  Some of the hardest hit areas included Newton (Sussex County) and Flemington (Hunterdon County).  Peak wind gusts included 55 mph in Harvey Cedars (Ocean County)  and at the McGuire AFB (Burlington County) and 53 mph in Belmar (Monmouth County) and Chatham (Morris County).  While it was also windy in the southern third of New Jersey, the highest wind gusts were a bit lower, generally between 35 mph and 45 mph.

Minor flooding of low-lying poor drainage areas occurred where leafs clogged drains.  In addition, northeast winds gusted from 25 to 35 mph.

The weekend of August 16th and 17th brought some of the hottest and most humid air of the summer into Central and Southern New Jersey.   High temperatures in most places reached well into the 90s, while dew point temperatures (an indication of how humid the air mass is) were as high as the mid 70s.  The apparent temperature or heat index on the 16th reached around 110 degrees.   The highest temperatures this weekend (on Saturday the 16th for most locations) included 100 degrees in Brick (Ocean County), 99 degrees at the McGuire AFB (Burlington County),  97 degrees in Somerdale (Camden County) and also at the Marina in Atlantic City on the 17th which was a new record,  96 degrees at the Atlantic City International Airport (on the 16th which tied a record) and 95 degrees in New Brunswick (Middlesex County), Mount Holly (Burlington County) and Wertsville (Hunterdon County).   The hot spell ended abruptly as a strong cold front moved through the region late on the 17th.  No serious heat related illnesses were reported.

High wind gusts developed along coastal Ocean County as a low pressure system intensified the night of the 20th.  A peak wind gust of 65 mph was reported at Seaside Park.  No serious wind related damage was reported.

Torrential rain fell across southeast New Jersey as a low pressure system developed over the Delmarva Peninsula and slowly moved northeast across southern New Jersey.  A series of thunderstorms developed along this low pressure system's frontal boundaries and trained or moved over the same areas. This caused  extremely heavy rain to fall over several hours across eastern parts of Atlantic County. The county bore the brunt of the storm and the flooding with storm totals in excess of 8 inches from Estell Manor through Galloway Township.  The storm total at the Atlantic City International Airport of 13.52 inches represented by far a greater than 100 year storm for the area.   A 100-year-storm for this area is 7.25 inches.  While total damage was estimated at 54 million dollars, miraculously the only injury occurred the afternoon of the 21st when a Galloway Township boy injured his back and neck diving into the flood waters.  Several major roadways and bridges collapsed or were completely washed out.  The governor declared a state of emergency for the county on the 21st and the county was eventually declared a federal disaster area.In Galloway Township, the hardest hit area, about 1,100 homes suffered damage.  This represented about 10 percent of all the township housing.  The most damage was occurred in the Pomona Oaks Development as 75 homes were badly flooded as a drainage pond filled and backed up.  The Osprey Court Development was also badly flooded.  The Atlantic City Medical Center was closed to emergencies as its first floor and basement were flooded.  Emergency personnel pumped out 1.8 million gallons of water from the center.  The adjacent Bacharach Rehabilitation Center was also flooded.  Flooding also damaged the Absegami High School and several facilities at the Richard Stockton College.The Atlantic City International Airport was closed at 1230 a.m. EDT on the 21st when power was knocked out to the runways and street flooding closed access to the airport.  The airport reopened at 1130 a.m. EDT on the 21st, but had to shut down again at sunset as the runway lights were still not working.  The FAA Technical Center was also closed.  In Hamilton Township, three major bridges were closed: The Sugar Hill Bridge on County Road 559, The Gravelly Run Bridge (County Road 559 also) on Ocean Heights Avenue and the bridge between the Lake Lenape Dam and the Great Harbor River.  Persons in homes near these bridges were evacuated to a senior citizen center.  Working around the clock, the Sugar Hill Bridge was repaired in time for the Labor Day Weekend.  The two other bridges were repaired by Late September.   Two New Jersey Transit buses and 40 passengers became stuck in the flood waters near the Hamilton Mall and had to be rescued.  Five thousand books in the Atlantic Community College were damaged by the flooding.  Parts of the Black Horse Pike (U.S. Route 40) were closed because the road washed out.   Sections of  U.S. Route 40 were also closed in Egg Harbor Township.  In Abesecon, a 180 foot section of the New Jersey Transit train track was closed after the gravel bed was washed away.  In Pleasantville, the motels around U.S. Routes 30 and 40 were evacuated because of the heavy rain and back bay flooding.  Several people had to be rescued from their vehicles.  At the water treatment plant pumping station, the sediment rate was above acceptable standards.  Even though Atlantic City proper escaped the heavy rain, residents had to boil their tap water for several days to make it potable because the city's reservoir in Egg Harbor Township flooded.  The United States Geological Survey Gage on the Tuckahoe River at Head of River reached a new record crest of 9.1 feet.  This represented a greater than 100 year recurrence interval.To put the storm total of 13.52 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport in perspective, the all-time certified 24 hour rainfall record for the state of New Jersey is 14.81 inches in Tuckerton (Ocean County) on August 19, 1939.  The 11.12 inches of rain that fell through midnight EST on the 20th, was a new all-time daily record.  The previous 24 hour record was 6.46 inches set on July 10, 1949.  The 13.52 inches also broke the PREVIOUS ALL-TIME MONTHLY RECORD of 13.09 inches set in July of 1959.  August 1997 would have a new rainfall record of 16.12 inches at the airport.  Other storm totals from Atlantic County included 12.7 inches in Mays Landing,  12.6 inches in Estell Manor, and 10.21 inches in Pleasantville.

Torrential rain fell across southeast New Jersey as a low pressure system developed over the Delmarva Peninsula and slowly moved northeast across southern New Jersey.  A series of thunderstorms developed along this low pressure system's frontal boundaries and trained or moved over the same areas. This caused extremely heavy rain to fall over several hours especially across southern parts of Ocean County.  Doppler Radar storm total estimates in Little Egg Harbor Township were between 8 and 10 inches.  Other storm totals included 6.75 inches in Beach Haven, 6 inches in Tom's River and 4.75 inches in East Dover.  Miraculously no serious injuries were reported.  Little Egg Harbor Township, especially on Mystic Island, reported the worst flooding in the county.  Vehicles were floating down the streets.  About 60 persons had to be evacuated to the Pinelands Middle School. About half of them were boy scouts in the Sea Pirate Campground.  Emergency personnel responded to 225 calls for help.  Lake Pohatcong overflowed and flooded U.S. Route 9 with 4 to 5 feet of water.  In Tuckerton, emergency personnel also responded to numerous calls for help.  Flooding was particularly bad along South Green and Center Streets.  In Stafford Township, several streets were impassable in the Ocean Acres and Beach Haven West neighborhoods.  Even an ambulance became stuck in flood waters.  In Dover Township, a home under construction collapsed because of the heavy rain and strong winds.  Damage was estimated at $200,000.  In Brick Township, water punched a hole through a berm around a retention basin and undermined road work along Brick Boulevard.  The most harrowing experience of the night was felt by a three boat party of 13 persons that were anchored near Little Egg Harbor.  As the winds increased, one boat broke away from its anchor and the three adults and two children aboard the "Little Lou" were tossed by strong waves without any radio contact.  The boat grounded on a sandbar and they were rescued about three hours later.

A  F0 (Fujita Scale) touched down briefly in Middletown Township and Highlands Borough before it went into Sandy Hook Bay and dissipated.   The path length was about 1.2 miles and the path width about 75 yards. The tornado damaged several cars and homes, and uprooted and/or snapped numerous trees, but no injuries were reported.  The tornado touched down in northeastern Middletown Township near Pape Drive and Navasink Avenue.   It moved northeast and uprooted a tree on Williams Street that crushed three parked cars.  Another car was burned when it came in contact with downed wires on Buttermilk Valley Road.  A tree also crushed an awning in the Shadow Lane Mobile Home Park.  In Highlands Borough,  a shed was blown off its foundation and carried by the tornado between two houses.  Other structural damage was mainly confined to broken windows, torn shingles and gutters.  Maximum wind speeds were estimated at the high end of the F0 scale at about 70 mph.
A severe thunderstorm blew over a shed and knocked down several trees that blocked one roadway in Galloway Township.

Thunderstorms with frequent lightning caused power outages and damage to electrical equipment in Berkeley, Dover and Manchester Townships.  Dover Township was hit the hardest with about 1,000 homes and businesses still without that night.  The heavy rain that accompanied this thunderstorm also helped cause a roadway to buckle in Toms River.

Thunderstorms with frequent lightning caused power outages and damage to electrical equipment in Berkeley, Dover and Manchester Townships.  Dover Township was hit the hardest with about 1,000 homes and businesses still without that night.  The heavy rain that accompanied this thunderstorm also helped cause a roadway to buckle in Toms River.
A severe thunderstorm pulled down several wires in the northwest part of Burlington County.  Most of the wind damage occurred around Pemberton.

The apparent temperature or heat index on the 16th reached around 110 degrees.   The highest temperatures this weekend (on Saturday the 16th for most locations) included 100 degrees in Brick (Ocean County), 99 degrees at the McGuire AFB (Burlington County),  97 degrees in Somerdale (Camden County) and also at the Marina in Atlantic City on the 17th which was a new record,  96 degrees at the Atlantic City International Airport (on the 16th which tied a record) and 95 degrees in New Brunswick (Middlesex County), Mount Holly (Burlington County) and Wertsville (Hunterdon County).   The hot spell ended abruptly as a strong cold front moved through the region late on the 17th.  No serious heat related illnesses were reported.
A thunderstorm caused numerous power outages in Wall Township.  Farther south in neighboring Ocean County, the same thunderstorm pulled down wires in Brick Township.
A severe thunderstorm pulled down trees and limbs in Holmdel.  A 34-year-old woman was killed when a tree fell down and crushed the vehicle she was driving in at 545 p.m. EDT.
Lightning struck and set the roof of a New Gretna house on fire.  No injuries were reported.
A severe thunderstorm pulled down trees and wires in and around Branchburg.  About 35,000 homes and businesses in the Public Service Electric and Gas service area in central New Jersey lost power.

Very strong onshore winds coupled with torrential rain, that nearly coincided with high tide along the back bays caused moderate tidal flooding along the barrier islands of Atlantic, Cape May and Ocean Counties.  The high tide at Atlantic City on the ocean side peaked at 7.7 feet above mean low water at about 11 p.m. EDT on the 20th.  This was a departure of about 2.7 feet above normal.  In Cape May County, street flooding was reported in the Wildwoods.  In Atlantic County,  widespread flooding occurred in Absecon at the intersection of U.S. Routes 9 and 30.  This flooded the entire inventory of Ditmare Motor Works and the Absecon True Value Hardware store at that intersection.  In Ocean County, moderate flooding was reported from Point Pleasant Beach to Seaside Park.  On Long Beach Island, Long Beach Township reported five cars became stuck in the rising water.  In Ship Bottom, four roads had to be closed because of tidal flooding.  Some roads were also closed in Surf City.  Heavy rain made it difficult to differentiate between the tidal and just poor drainage flooding, especially in Atlantic and Ocean Counties.  While heavier rain fell inland storm totals along the coast included 6.75 inches in Beach Haven,  6.6 inches in Harvey Cedars,  5.51 inches at the Marina in Atlantic City and 4.55 inches in Margate.  In addition to the flooding,  some beach erosion did occur in Atlantic City and Cape May City.  The erosion was bad enough to totally remove the sand and expose the large rocks that were covered by the previous beach replenishment project in Cape May City.

Torrential rain fell across southeast New Jersey as a low pressure system developed over the Delmarva Peninsula and slowly moved northeast across southern New Jersey.  A series of thunderstorms developed along this low pressure system's frontal boundaries and trained or moved over the same areas. This caused torrential rain to fall for several hours across the extreme southeast tip of Burlington County.    Doppler Radar storm total estimated 6 to 8 inches of rain fell across the extreme southeast tip of the county.  Storm totals decreased quickly farther northwest.  Only 3.48 inches of rain fell in Atsion, 3.43 inches in New Lisbon and 1.51 inches in Mount Holly.  The torrential rain helped set a new record stage height for the East Branch of the Bass River at New Gretna.  The river crested at 7.28 feet.  The previous record was 6.36 feet on July 4, 1978.  According to the United States Geological Survey, this represents a recurrence interval of greater than 100 years.  Miraculously no major damage or injuries occurred.

Strong winds preceding a developing low pressure system caused winds to gust to around 50 mph along the Atlantic County shore.  A peak wind gust of 49 mph was reported at the Marina in Atlantic City at 1245 a.m. EDT on the 21st.  A tent erected for a revival meeting within the city was destroyed by the wind.

A 100 acre wild fire in Parsippany forced the evacuation of 354 students from the Troy Hills Elementary School and injured a fire fighter battling the blaze.  The fire began about noon EST in the Troy Meadows Preserve in seven separate locations.  Authorities suspected arson.  The run of dry weather throughout 1997 helped the fire spread quicker.  Police blocked several roads near the preserve.  The fire was declared under control at 3 p.m. EST.

The weekend of August 16th and 17th brought some of the hottest and most humid air of the summer into Central and Southern New Jersey.   High temperatures in most places reached well into the 90s, while dew point temperatures (an indication of how humid the air mass is) were as high as the mid 70s.  The apparent temperature or heat index on the 16th reached around 110 degrees.   The highest temperatures this weekend (on Saturday the 16th for most locations) included 100 degrees in Brick (Ocean County), 99 degrees at the McGuire AFB (Burlington County),  97 degrees in Somerdale (Camden County) and also at the Marina in Atlantic City on the 17th which was a new record,  96 degrees at the Atlantic City International Airport (on the 16th which tied a record) and 95 degrees in New Brunswick (Middlesex County), Mount Holly (Burlington County) and Wertsville (Hunterdon County).   The hot spell ended abruptly as a strong cold front moved through the region late on the 17th.  No serious heat related illnesses were reported.

A vigorous cold front moved through New Jersey during the early morning on the 27th and was accompanied by strong gusty winds.  The strongest gusts occurred during two surges,  one within a couple of hours of the cold frontal passage.  The second (and in most areas just as strong) surge occurred during the late morning on the 27th.  At about 1120 a.m. EST, a 31-year-old woman was killed when she was struck in the head by a falling tree limb and knocked off her horse during a fox hunt on the Ellisdale Road property of Princeton Nurseries in Upper Freehold Township in Monmouth County.  The strong winds pulled down tree limbs and this resulted in power outages to about 20,000 homes throughout the northern half of New Jersey.  Some of the hardest hit areas included Newton (Sussex County) and Flemington (Hunterdon County).  Peak wind gusts included 55 mph in Harvey Cedars (Ocean County)  and at the McGuire AFB (Burlington County) and 53 mph in Belmar (Monmouth County) and Chatham (Morris County).  While it was also windy in the southern third of New Jersey, the highest wind gusts were a bit lower, generally between 35 mph and 45 mph.
A severe thunderstorm pulled down wires in Neptune.

Showers and thunderstorms with heavy rain caused some urban and poor drainage flooding in Northwest New Jersey.  The heaviest rain fell in two bursts: during the morning commute hours and again during the mid afternoon.  Storm totals averaged between 2.0 and 3.5 inches.  Two children were injured in Byram Township when a rotten tree limb from a dead tree fell onto them while they were waiting for a school bus.  Storm totals included 4.33 inches in Montague (Sussex County),  3.53 inches in Morristown (Morris County), 3.28 inches in both Rockaway (Morris County)  and White House Station (Hunterdon County),  2.91 inches in Oak Ridge (Morris County),  2.81 inches in Wantage (Sussex County),  2.53 inches in Long Valley (Morris County),  2.35 inches in Chatham (Morris County), 2.28 inches in Belvidere (Warren County) and 2.07 inches in Sussex (Sussex County).

A coastal storm or nor'easter developed along the South Carolina coast the morning of the 13th and moved slowly northeast and was about 75 miles east of Salisbury Maryland at 7 a.m. EST on the 14th.  The low pressure system then accelerated and was well southeast of Nova Scotia on the morning of the 15th.  This nor'easter unfortunately coincided with the full moon and spring tides and caused moderate tidal flooding during the morning high tide and minor tidal flooding during the evening high tide on the 14th.  In addition, the onshore flow for several tide cycles caused beach erosion, that was severe again in Sea Isle City.Cape May County again was hit the hardest by this nor'easter.  In Sea Isle City about 290,000 cubic yards of beach was lost due to erosion, nearly a fourth of this occurred in the south end beaches from 89th Street to the Townsend's Inlet Bridge.  Seventy truckloads of fill brought in after the previous week's nor'easter were all washed away.  Water came within about five feet of the Tiburon Shores Condominiums and wood pylons became exposed.  Beach damage was described as "considerable" at the Cape May Point Beach.  Cars were submerged at high tide in both Ocean City and Cape May.  Two roads in Wildwood were closed because of tidal flooding.In Monmouth County, tidal flooding caused street closures in Highlands, Keyport and Sandy Hook.  In Ocean County, Harvey Cedars reported severe erosion and also lost some dune fencing.   Barnegat Bay tidal flooding also occurred on Cedar Bonnet Island, Little Egg Harbor Township and Tuckerton..  In Atlantic County, the Eastbound lanes of the Black and White Horse Pikes (U.S. Routes 30 and 40) were closed during the morning of the 14th and traffic had to be rerouted into the city.  Bay flooding also forced the closure of Albany Avenue in Atlantic City and West End Avenue in Ventnor and Atlantic City.   The high tides Friday (the 14th) morning were only about 2 feet above normal.  But the onshore flow coincided with relatively high astronomical tides (even by spring tide standards) and produced moderate tidal flooding.  Tides reached 7.8 feet above mean low water at Sandy Hook,  7.5 feet above mean low water in Atlantic City and 7.9 feet above mean low water in Cape May.

An intense low pressure system that moved east of Cape Hatteras in combination with relatively high astronomical tides coming off the full moon on Wednesday the 15th produced minor tidal flooding around the time of the midday high tide on the 19th.  Two low pressure systems formed off the South Atlantic Coast on the 18th: one near Cape Hatteras, the other just southeast of the South Carolina Coast.  They merged into one during the morning of the 19th east of Cape Hatteras.  During the next 24 hours, this low deepened rapidly (or bombed) from 1000 mb the morning of the 19th to 976 mb the morning of the 20th.  Fortunately for the New Jersey coast, this low moved eastnortheast and not northeast.  By the morning of the 20th, it was already 780 miles east of the New Jersey coastline.     The highest tides associated with this low occurred during the midday high tide on Sunday the 19th. This caused some minor tidal flooding.  The high tide in Atlantic City was 7.0 feet above mean low water and in Wildwood 7.5 feet above mean low water.  Several streets in North Wildwood were closed because of the tidal flooding.  The persistent northeast flow for a couple of days also caused rough surf and some beach erosion.  The United States Coast Guard rescued a single male about 3/4 of a mile off the Ocean City coast.

A gust front from a squall line of severe thunderstorms knocked down trees in Edison.  As the thunderstorms themselves moved across central Middlesex County, they reintensified and proceeded to cause scattered power outages mainly in the eastern half of the county as they downed more trees and wires. About 35,000 homes and businesses in the Public Service Electric and Gas service area in central New Jersey lost power on the 16th; 4,000 still did not have power restored by the evening of the 17th.

Also, the salt line in the Delaware River, was located near Bridgeport in Gloucester County, approximately four miles farther north than it should have been for late October.  The yearly precipitation totals on a county weighted average were below normal in all of New Jersey except for Atlantic, Cape May and Cumberland Counties.  Precipitation deficits through October 31st averaged around 5 inches in the other counties.  The greatest deficits were in Salem (-7.2 inches), Morris (-7.1 inches) and Somerset (-6.0 inches) Counties.  In the latter three counties, this was about 80 percent of the normal precipitation.  Because a large number of New Jersey farms have irrigation capabilities, agricultural losses from the drought were not as severe as they were in Maryland and Pennsylvania.

A slow moving nor'easter caused strong winds, beach erosion and widespread, but minor, tidal flooding from mid-day on the 7th through the afternoon on the 8th.  The low pressure system formed near Cape Hatteras on the morning of the 7th and moved slowly north as the surface low was captured by a closed low at higher levels of the atmosphere.  On the morning of the 8th,  it was located about 80 miles east of Wallops Island Virginia and on the morning of the 9th,  it was near Cape May City.  That morning a second low pressure system was located near Cape Cod Massachusetts and the two merged into one and was located just south of Nova Scotia by the morning of the 10th.  The low came close enough to the New Jersey shore by the morning of the 9th to cause the onshore winds and consequently the tidal flooding to diminish.The several tide cycle pounding cause beach erosion along the New Jersey Coast and it became severe in a few locations.  In Cape May County, in Sea Isle City, severe erosion occurred from 89th through 93rd Streets and the ocean came within 15 feet of the Tiburon Shores Condominiums.  The surf removed dunes and steps in that area.  In Ocean County, in Harvey Cedars, the pounding surf scoured a six foot cliff north of Bergen Avenue.  In Brant Beach, waves crashed close to the dunes on  41st Street.  In Monmouth County, the surf removed about 50 to 100 feet of beach in Manasquan and created a four foot cliff in Spring Lake. The persistent onshore flow raised tidal departures to 3.0 to 3.5 feet above normal and around 2.5 feet above normal at times of high tide from mid-day on the 7th (Friday) through the afternoon on the 8th (Saturday).  This caused widespread, but minor,  tidal flooding from the mid-day high tide on the 7th through the afternoon on the 8th.   The highest tides reported were 6.6 feet above mean low water in Atlantic City, 6.9 feet above mean low water in Sandy Hook and 7.4 feet above mean low water in Cape May City.  In Monmouth County, tidal flooding was reported along the Shrewsbury River in Rumson and Sea Bright.  In Ocean County, in Beach Haven, minor tidal flooding occurred along Bay Avenue and minor flooding was also reported in Barnegat.  In Atlantic County, in Absecon, the intersection of  U.S. Route 9 and Shore Road were closed for several hours the evening of the 7th.  Some bayside blocks in southern Atlantic City were flooded.  In Cape May County, tidal flooding forced the closure of several blocks in Ocean City and Wildwood. Strong gusty winds buffeted the region from the morning of the 7th through the afternoon of the 8th.  The strongest gusts were reported during the afternoon and evening of the 7th.  Peak gusts included 59 mph in Williamstown (Gloucester County), 56 mph in Seaside Park and Harvey Cedars (both Ocean County), 47 mph at the Marina in Atlantic City, 45 mph in Belmar and 44 mph in Wildwood.  In Ocean County, the strong winds caused power outages to about 5,000 homes in Stafford Township and ripped away beach fencing in Beach Haven.  During the same storm, a ship reported a severe thunderstorm that produced wind gusts to 86 mph at 10 p.m. EST on the 8th about 100 nautical miles east of Cape May County.

On the morning of the 8th,  it was located about 80 miles east of Wallops Island Virginia and on the morning of the 9th,  it was near Cape May City.  That morning a second low pressure system was located near Cape Cod Massachusetts and the two merged into one and was located just south of Nova Scotia by the morning of the 10th.  The low came close enough to the New Jersey shore by the morning of the 9th to cause the onshore winds and consequently the tidal flooding to diminish.The several tide cycle pounding cause beach erosion along the New Jersey Coast and it became severe in a few locations.  In Cape May County, in Sea Isle City, severe erosion occurred from 89th through 93rd Streets and the ocean came within 15 feet of the Tiburon Shores Condominiums. In Brant Beach, waves crashed close to the dunes on  41st Street.  In Monmouth County, the surf removed about 50 to 100 feet of beach in Manasquan and created a four foot cliff in Spring Lake. The persistent onshore flow raised tidal departures to 3.0 to 3.5 feet above normal and around 2.5 feet above normal at times of high tide from mid-day on the 7th (Friday) through the afternoon on the 8th (Saturday). A coastal storm or nor'easter developed along the South Carolina coast the morning of the 13th and moved slowly northeast and was about 75 miles east of Salisbury Maryland at 7 a.m. EST on the 14th.  The low pressure system then accelerated and was well southeast of Nova Scotia on the morning of the 15th.  Preceding this low pressure was an unseasonably cold and dry air mass.  This factor coupled with the northeast flow around the coastal low and the presence of a new and fresh supply of cold air supplied by another high pressure system in nearby Canada kept temperatures near the ground at or below the freezing mark and caused precipitation to fall as mainly freezing rain in Sussex County,  in northern areas of Warren County and northwest sections of Morris County.  The freezing rain contributed to a fatal accident in Sussex County as a 49-year-old man was killed at 147 p.m. EST when he lost control of his car and slammed into a utility pole on U.S. Route 206 in Sandyston Township.Precipitation started around midnight on the 14th and lasted most of the day.  Precipitation ended as some light snow across much of the area, especially in Sussex County where 1 to 3 inches accumulated on top of the ice in the northwest hills.  Across Hunterdon, Middlesex and Somerset Counties, precipitation started briefly as freezing rain before going over to plain rain.  The rain then change back to freezing rain across northern parts of the counties before it ended late on the 14th. Ice accretions averaged around half an inch, but reached close to one inch in the northwest corner of Sussex County.  The ice brought down tree limbs and power lines and caused power outages to 2,000 customers across Northwest New Jersey.  A downed tree in Randolph Township (Morris County) caused the greatest concentration of outages in one area.   The freezing rain contributed to numerous accidents across Northwest New Jersey.  In Newton (Sussex County) alone twice vehicles were involved in head-on collisions after they slid into oncoming traffic.  In Morris County, a Morris Township bus driver fractured both legs after the bus slid off a driveway into a tree.  Two children were also injured.  In Denville, a car slid into a guide wire, while in Parsippany, a van flipped onto its roof.

A vigorous cold front moved through New Jersey during the early morning on the 27th and was accompanied by strong gusty winds.  The strongest gusts occurred during two surges,  one within a couple of hours of the cold frontal passage.  The second (and in most areas just as strong) surge occurred during the late morning on the 27th.  At about 1120 a.m. EST, a 31-year-old woman was killed when she was struck in the head by a falling tree limb and knocked off her horse during a fox hunt on the Ellisdale Road property of Princeton Nurseries in Upper Freehold Township in Monmouth County.  The strong winds pulled down tree limbs and this resulted in power outages to about 20,000 homes throughout the northern half of New Jersey.  Some of the hardest hit areas included Newton (Sussex County) and Flemington (Hunterdon County).  Peak wind gusts included 55 mph in Harvey Cedars (Ocean County)  and at the McGuire AFB (Burlington County) and 53 mph in Belmar (Monmouth County) and Chatham (Morris County).  While it was also windy in the southern third of New Jersey, the highest wind gusts were a bit lower, generally between 35 mph and 45 mph.

The low pressure system then accelerated and was well southeast of Nova Scotia on the morning of the 15th.  This nor'easter unfortunately coincided with the full moon and spring tides and caused moderate tidal flooding during the morning high tide and minor tidal flooding during the evening high tide on the 14th.  In addition, the onshore flow for several tide cycles caused beach erosion, that was severe again in Sea Isle City.Cape May County again was hit the hardest by this nor'easter.  In Sea Isle City about 290,000 cubic yards of beach was lost due to erosion, nearly a fourth of this occurred in the south end beaches from 89th Street to the Townsend's Inlet Bridge.  Seventy truckloads of fill brought in after the previous week's nor'easter were all washed away.  Water came within about five feet of the Tiburon Shores Condominiums and wood pylons became exposed.  Beach damage was described as "considerable" at the Cape May Point Beach.  Cars were submerged at high tide in both Ocean City and Cape May.  Two roads in Wildwood were closed because of tidal flooding.In Monmouth County, tidal flooding caused street closures in Highlands, Keyport and Sandy Hook.  In Ocean County, Harvey Cedars reported severe erosion and also lost some dune fencing.   Barnegat Bay tidal flooding also occurred on Cedar Bonnet Island, Little Egg Harbor Township and Tuckerton..  In Atlantic County, the Eastbound lanes of the Black and White Horse Pikes (U.S. Routes 30 and 40) were closed during the morning of the 14th and traffic had to be rerouted into the city.  Bay flooding also forced the closure of Albany Avenue in Atlantic City and West End Avenue in Ventnor and Atlantic City.   The high tides Friday (the 14th) morning were only about 2 feet above normal.  But the onshore flow coincided with relatively high astronomical tides (even by spring tide standards) and produced moderate tidal flooding.  Tides reached 7.8 feet above mean low water at Sandy Hook,  7.5 feet above mean low water in Atlantic City and 7.9 feet above mean low water in Cape May.

In Ocean County, Harvey Cedars reported severe erosion and also lost some dune fencing.   Barnegat Bay tidal flooding also occurred on Cedar Bonnet Island, Little Egg Harbor Township and Tuckerton..  In Atlantic County, the Eastbound lanes of the Black and White Horse Pikes (U.S. Routes 30 and 40) were closed during the morning of the 14th and traffic had to be rerouted into the city.  Bay flooding also forced the closure of Albany Avenue in Atlantic City and West End Avenue in Ventnor and Atlantic City.   The high tides Friday (the 14th) morning were only about 2 feet above normal.  But the onshore flow coincided with relatively high astronomical tides (even by spring tide standards) and produced moderate tidal flooding.  Tides reached 7.8 feet above mean low water at Sandy Hook,  7.5 feet above mean low water in Atlantic City and 7.9 feet above mean low water in Cape May.

Unseasonably dry weather with below normal rainfall, which became worse during the summer months, forced the Delaware River Basin Commission to declare a drought warning on October 27th.  The commission urged the 7 million residents within the basin's 13,539 square mile area to voluntarily conserve water.  Water levels in the New York City Reservoirs, which are in the headwaters of the Delaware River, fell below 40 percent of capacity in late October.  Also, the salt line in the Delaware River, was located near Bridgeport in Gloucester County, approximately four miles farther north than it should have been for late October.  The yearly precipitation totals on a county weighted average were below normal in all of New Jersey except for Atlantic, Cape May and Cumberland Counties.  Precipitation deficits through October 31st averaged around 5 inches in the other counties.  The greatest deficits were in Salem (-7.2 inches), Morris (-7.1 inches) and Somerset (-6.0 inches) Counties.  In the latter three counties, this was about 80 percent of the normal precipitation.  Because a large number of New Jersey farms have irrigation capabilities, agricultural losses from the drought were not as severe as they were in Maryland and Pennsylvania.

A coastal storm developed off the Carolina Coast during Saturday morning, November 1st.  It moved north and passed over the Delaware Coast around 3 pm and over the local area during the evening.The storm brought strong gusty winds and rain to the area.East winds increased to 20 to 30 mph with gusts from 40 to 45 mph during the afternoon.  The highest wind gust was 44 mph at West Orange (Essex County).  Strong gusty winds combined with rain and downed scattered tree limbs, trees, and power lines.  An 85-foot oak tree fell and damaged 3 fences and a house deck on Franklin Road in Englewood (Bergen County).Rainfall amounts ranged from 0.82 inches at Newark Airport  to 1.83 inches at West Orange (Essex  County).

The weekend of August 16th and 17th brought some of the hottest and most humid air of the summer into Central and Southern New Jersey.   High temperatures in most places reached well into the 90s, while dew point temperatures (an indication of how humid the air mass is) were as high as the mid 70s.  The apparent temperature or heat index on the 16th reached around 110 degrees.   The highest temperatures this weekend (on Saturday the 16th for most locations) included 100 degrees in Brick (Ocean County), 99 degrees at the McGuire AFB (Burlington County),  97 degrees in Somerdale (Camden County) and also at the Marina in Atlantic City on the 17th which was a new record,  96 degrees at the Atlantic City International Airport (on the 16th which tied a record) and 95 degrees in New Brunswick (Middlesex County), Mount Holly (Burlington County) and Wertsville (Hunterdon County).   The hot spell ended abruptly as a strong cold front moved through the region late on the 17th.  No serious heat related illnesses were reported.

Very strong onshore winds coupled with torrential rain, that nearly coincided with high tide along the back bays caused moderate tidal flooding along the barrier islands of Atlantic, Cape May and Ocean Counties.  The high tide at Atlantic City on the ocean side peaked at 7.7 feet above mean low water at about 11 p.m. EDT on the 20th.  This was a departure of about 2.7 feet above normal.  In Cape May County, street flooding was reported in the Wildwoods.  In Atlantic County,  widespread flooding occurred in Absecon at the intersection of U.S. Routes 9 and 30.  This flooded the entire inventory of Ditmare Motor Works and the Absecon True Value Hardware store at that intersection.  In Ocean County, moderate flooding was reported from Point Pleasant Beach to Seaside Park.  On Long Beach Island, Long Beach Township reported five cars became stuck in the rising water.  In Ship Bottom, four roads had to be closed because of tidal flooding.  Some roads were also closed in Surf City.  Heavy rain made it difficult to differentiate between the tidal and just poor drainage flooding, especially in Atlantic and Ocean Counties.  While heavier rain fell inland storm totals along the coast included 6.75 inches in Beach Haven,  6.6 inches in Harvey Cedars,  5.51 inches at the Marina in Atlantic City and 4.55 inches in Margate.  In addition to the flooding,  some beach erosion did occur in Atlantic City and Cape May City.  The erosion was bad enough to totally remove the sand and expose the large rocks that were covered by the previous beach replenishment project in Cape May City.

An intense low pressure system that moved east of Cape Hatteras in combination with relatively high astronomical tides coming off the full moon on Wednesday the 15th produced minor tidal flooding around the time of the midday high tide on the 19th.  Two low pressure systems formed off the South Atlantic Coast on the 18th: one near Cape Hatteras, the other just southeast of the South Carolina Coast.  They merged into one during the morning of the 19th east of Cape Hatteras.  During the next 24 hours, this low deepened rapidly (or bombed) from 1000 mb the morning of the 19th to 976 mb the morning of the 20th.  Fortunately for the New Jersey coast, this low moved eastnortheast and not northeast.  By the morning of the 20th, it was already 780 miles east of the New Jersey coastline.     The highest tides associated with this low occurred during the midday high tide on Sunday the 19th. This caused some minor tidal flooding.  The high tide in Atlantic City was 7.0 feet above mean low water and in Wildwood 7.5 feet above mean low water.  Several streets in North Wildwood were closed because of the tidal flooding.  The persistent northeast flow for a couple of days also caused rough surf and some beach erosion.  The United States Coast Guard rescued a single male about 3/4 of a mile off the Ocean City coast.
